codeReview.summary: The migration refactor correctly centralizes favorites into the canonical user_favorites document and adds strong regression coverage for one-time legacy merges, but it still drops legacy identifier metadata and silently freezes migration for users whose legacy data is temporarily unreadable after the first canonical touch.

issues.description: Once a canonical document is finalized, `_ensure_canonical_favorites` returns immediately whenever legacy rows are currently absent, so a user who completes migration while `users.favorite_stocks` is temporarily empty/noisy can never recover late-visible legacy favorites. That violates the requirement to preserve pre-existing user data during migration under transient visibility issues.

`_normalize_favorite` rewrites every record to a fixed canonical subset (`stock_code`, `stock_name`, `market`, timestamps, tags, notes, alert prices) and discards any other existing metadata fields from legacy or canonical entries. The feature contract explicitly requires metadata semantics to stay stable, so fields outside this allowlist are lost during migration and subsequent CRUD updates.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The canonical watchlist contract now depends on a one-time migration marker protocol (`canonical_version`, `legacy_migrated_at`, `legacy_migration_checked_at`) to control retries and dedupe behavior, but neither AGENTS.md nor .factory/library/architecture.md documents those persisted fields or the expected retry semantics.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: app/services/favorites_service.py:164-287 introduces these markers as migration invariants; mission guidance only says to preserve legacy data exactly once.

summary: Reviewed handoff, transcript skeleton, skill guidance, and commit `c42f32a2`. The feature substantially improves canonical favorites migration and coverage, but it still has two blocking contract misses: finalized migrations cannot recover legacy data if it becomes visible only after a later outage/empty read, and the normalization path strips arbitrary legacy metadata fields instead of preserving them.
